IT Fundamentals (CSGE501) Generic Elective - (GE) 
Credit: 06 
 
Course Objectives 
This course introduces the students to the basic concepts of computers. The aim is to bridge 
the fundamental concepts of computers with the present level of knowledge of the students. 
The course also aims to skill the students so that they can make use of information 
technology effectively in all walks of life. 
 
Course Learning Outcomes 
On successful completion of this course, students will be able to: 
1. develop a vocabulary of key terms related to the computer and to software program 
menus, identify the components of a personal computer system and use the interface 
deftly. 
2. organize files and documents on storage devices. 
3. compose, format and edit a word document. 
4. use spreadsheet for storing data and performing preliminary analysis. 
5. acquire fundamental knowledge of networking and distinguish between different 
types of networks. 
6. acquire knowledge of internet applications and use them. 
 
Detailed Syllabus 
Unit 1 
Introduction: Introduction to logical organization of computer, input and output devices, 
keyboard, mouse, joystick, scanner, OCR, OMR, monitor, Printer, Plotter. 
Unit 2 
Storage Devices: Primary memory, secondary memory, auxiliary memory. 
Unit 3 
User Interface: Operating system as user interface, system tools, control panel settings.

Unit 4 Database: Introduction to database, use of spreadsheet as database, use of functions and database operations in spreadsheet. Unit 5 Networks: Definition of network, classification of network, LAN, MAN, WAN, distinction among networks. Unit 6 Internet Applications: Internet as a global network, Internet utilities – email, online banking, reservations etc. Practical Text Editor 1. Multimedia and Web Design (CSGE601) Generic Elective - (GE) 
Credit: 06 
 
Course Objective 
This paper aims to provide comprehensive review of concepts and techniques for designing 
and developing attractive websites with multimedia components. 
 
Course Learning Outcomes 
On successful completion of the course, students will be able to: 
● understand fundamental Web design principles and technologies· 
● understand the detailed design plan required to create a successful Web site that consi
ders audience needs, accessibility features, and various technical issues. 
● understand the coverage of ownership, permissions, and copyright issues· 
● incorporate text, images, animation, sound, and video into Web pages. 
● create a website with popular multimedia authoring tools, such as Macromedia Flash.

Unit 6 HTML: Introduction to hypertext markup language, document type definition, creating web pages, graphical elements, lists, hyperlinks, tables, web forms, inserting images, inserting audio, video and animation; frames, use of CSS. Data Science using R (CSGE602) Generic Elective - (GE) 
Credit: 06 
 
Course Objective 
Data Science also known as data driven science makes use of scientific methods, processes 
and systems to extract knowledge or insights from data in various forms (structured or 
unstructured). The study of these methods and processes is the main objective of the course. 
Course Learning Outcomes 
On successful completion of the course, students will be able to: 
1. create effective solutions to data analysis challenges. 
2. effectively organize and manage data science projects. 
3. analyse problems and identify solutions. 
4. communicate data science problems, methods, and findings effectively, visually, and 
in writing.

Detailed Syllabus 
Unit 1 
Introduction: Introduction to Data Science, Data Science Basics, Need of Data Science, Life 
Cycle of Data Science, Challenges of Data Science Technology, Data Science Components, 
,Applications of Data Science, Data Science Job Roles, Tools for Data Science, Structured 
Data, Unstructured Data. 
Unit 2 
R Programming Basics: Overview of R, R data types and objects, Operators, Precedence 
and associativity, Decision Making, Loops, Functions, Scoping, File System, Reading and 
writing Data 
Unit 3 
Data Structures In R programming: Strings, Vectors, Lists, Matrices, Arrays, 
Factors, Data Frames. 
 
Unit 4 
Statistical Analysis: Mean, Harmonic Mean, Median, Mode, Standard Deviation, Central 
tendency, measures of dispersion, Data Distribution, Analysis of Variance & covariance, 
Summary Function, Z score Function, Linear Regression analysis, Multiple Regression, 
Logistic regression. 
Unit 5 
Data Visualization: Overview of Data Visualization, Pie Chart, Line Graphs, Bar Chart, 
Histogram, Box plot, Scatter Plot, Function plot, Animating objects using R. 
Unit 6 
Case Study: Suitable Case Studies will be taken up. The list will be provided by the 
department in consultation with the college teachers in the beginning of the semester. 
 
Practical 
 
Q1. Write an R script to do the following: 
a) Draw 10 random letters from the lowercase alphabet, and sort them alphabetically. 
b) Draw 5 random letters from each of the lowercase and uppercase alphabets, 
incorporating both into a single vector, and sort it alphabetically. 
c) Repeat the above exercise but sort the vector alphabetically in descending order.

Q2. Read a text file that contains sales of milk in litres for 15 days in three different shops (the first 3 values are for shops 1,2 and 3 on Monday, etc.). Produce a statistical summary of the sales for each day of the week and also for each shop. Q3. Read the rainfall in cm of three different states S1, S2 and S3 from a CSV file. Write an R script to plot an appropriate graph to compare them using different colours. Label the graph, x-axis, y-axis and the legend. Q4. Read a text file containing the monthly expenditure breakdown of an individual as {Housing, Food, Clothing, entertainment, Others}. Write an R script to: a) represent monthly expenditure breakdown using a pie chart. b) represent the percentage of each expenditure using pie chart. Also, add chart legend to the graph. Q5. Write an R script to do the following: a) Simulate a sample of 100 random data points from a normal distribution with mean 100 and standard deviation 5, and store the result in a vector. b) Plot a histogram and a boxplot of the vector you just created. c) Calculate the summary of the sample generated. d) Using the data above, test the hypothesis that the mean equals 100. e) Test the hypothesis that mean equals 90. Use data set of your choice from Open Data Portal (https://data.gov.in/) for the following exercises. Q6. Read the all rows from the CSV file, with header. Write an R script to:

the data above, test the hypothesis that the mean equals 100. e) Test the hypothesis that mean equals 90. Use data set of your choice from Open Data Portal (https://data.gov.in/) for the following exercises. Q6. Read the all rows from the CSV file, with header. Write an R script to: a) create a subset of the data records that satisfy a condition. b) find suitable descriptive statistics for each column. c) draw boxplot for the numeric attributes and identify outliers, if any d) find correlation for each pair of numeric attributes, and draw scatter plot matrix. e) draw histograms, pie charts for categorical attributes. f) draw the probability density curve for numeric attributes. Over the past three decades, Maharaja Agrasen College, University of Delhi has distinguished itself as one of the premier academic institutions in the country. With state-of-the-art infrastructure and vibrant intellectual spaces, the college vitalises critical thinking, numerous participatory discussions and debates, and a holistic development and an inclusive environment. Its devoted faculty, acclaimed for academic rigour and professional integrity, its efficient administrative support, and its verdant, nurturing campus environment together create a space that inspires both cognitive growth and ethical and creative stimulation. The college boasts advanced learning facilities including smart classrooms, a modern auditorium, ICT-enabled rooms, a conference hall, and a well-stocked library equipped with electronic resources. Students benefit from amenities such as a gymnasium, meticulously maintained sports grounds, and Vidyottama, the residential accommodation for female students. A noteworthy recent addition is Sahastradhara, an open micro-learning platform designed to enhance creativity and innovation. A wide spectrum of co-curricular committees, clubs, centres, and departmental initiatives manifest academic engagement with dynamic skill-building. These forums cultivate leadership, teamwork, and organisational, and collaborative skills, offering students to widen the democratic avenues for intellectual and creative expression, and empower them with new perspectives and bold ideas. The college's Environment Cell, through its dynamic and proactive sustainability efforts, has earned MAC the distinction of being a zero-waste campus. Whether through its green ethos or its comprehensive support for professional and socio-ethical growth seen in a range of platforms like the NCC Girls Cadets, Placement Cell, Abdul Kalam Centre of Research, Centre for Civic Education, Centre

proactive sustainability efforts, has earned MAC the distinction of being a zero-waste campus. Whether through its green ethos or its comprehensive support for professional and socio-ethical growth seen in a range of platforms like the NCC Girls Cadets, Placement Cell, Abdul Kalam Centre of Research, Centre for Civic Education, Centre for Performing Arts and Cultural Studies, Sanskriti Kala Kendra, and various student societies, MAC offers a rich, motivating ecosystem for young learners to evolve into responsible and enlightened citizens. At Maharaja Agrasen College, you will find an empowering space to explore creativity, engage in original research, build your academic aptitude, participate in insightful dialogues on social relevance, and contribute significantly towards a sustainable and inclusive future. I extend my heartfelt wishes for your continued success, growth, and openness to the many possibilities that await you all as you embark on this new chapter of your lives. Prof. Rakesh Kumar Chairman, Governing Body

Prof. Sanjeev Kumar Tiwari Principal It gives me immense joy to welcome all students joining Maharaja Agrasen College for the academic session 2025–26. I congratulate each one of you for clearing the CUET examination and securing a place in one of the most vibrant institutions of Delhi University. This marks the beginning of a journey filled with growth, discovery, and purpose. This year is particularly significant as the fourth year of undergraduate education, under the framework of the National Education Policy (NEP), has been officially rolled out. This reform is a major stride toward promoting flexible, research-driven, and multidisciplinary education. The NEP also emphasizes vocational training and skill development to prepare youths for real-world challenges. As Bharat emerges as the 4th largest economy in the world, the responsibility of sustaining and advancing this growth lies with its youth. The knowledge and skills you acquire here will shape not just your individual future but also the future of our nation. As Swami Vivekananda rightly said, "Education is the manifestation of the perfection already in man." We believe in nurturing that potential through value-based, inclusive, and forward-looking education. Maharaja Agrasen College, accredited with an ‘A’ Grade, proudly ranked 23rd in Science stream, 26th in Arts and 35th in Commerce in the MDRA-India Today College Rankings 2025, continues to be a symbol of academic excellence. Our 10-acre campus houses state-of-the-art infrastructure which includes modern laboratories, an advanced ICT Centre, a fully equipped auditorium, spacious conference halls, a rich library with over 50,000 titles,

26th in Arts and 35th in Commerce in the MDRA-India Today College Rankings 2025, continues to be a symbol of academic excellence. Our 10-acre campus houses state-of-the-art infrastructure which includes modern laboratories, an advanced ICT Centre, a fully equipped auditorium, spacious conference halls, a rich library with over 50,000 titles, and smart, interactive classrooms. Our faculty has enthusiastically embraced the NEP’s vision, undertaking numerous research projects and contributing to an intellectually stimulating environment. Dedicated centres such as the Abdul Kalam Centre, Centre for Performing Arts and Culture, Sanskriti Kala Kendra, and Civic Education Centre further encourage student-led innovation and inquiry. Beyond academics, our commitment to holistic development is unwavering. Through the NSS and the NCC-Girls Unit, students actively participate in social initiatives and national programs. The Extra Curricular Activities Committee, with its 14 dynamic societies, offers ample opportunities to cultivate artistic and cultural talents. The MAC-Wellness Centre and our expansive sports infrastructure support physical well-being and team spirit. As you embark on this transformative journey, always keep in mind that you are stepping into a space that not only imparts knowledge but also shapes character. Make the most of it. Maharaja Agrasen College (MAC), in just three decades since its inception, has emerged as one of India’s premier institutions for higher education in the Arts, Science, and Commerce streams. With a commitment to academic excellence and allround development, the college has consistently excelled in academics, sports, extracurricular achievements, and faculty advancement. It proudly retained its NAAC Grade ‘A’ in the 2022 cycle, reflecting its unwavering pursuit of quality education. In the recent national survey by the India Today Group and MDRA, MAC was ranked 23th in Science, 26th in Arts, and 35th in Commerce—a testament to its growing prominence. Furthermore, its inclusion among the top 50 institutions in the National Institutional Ranking Framework (2022) by the Ministry of Education stands as a marker of its excellence. This success is driven by a dedicated faculty that integrates cuttingedge research and innovative pedagogies to nurture young minds. The college remains at the forefront of educational innovation, constantly adapting to the latest technologies and teaching methodologies to foster a holistic learning environment. Even during the global pandemic, MAC rose to the challenge by launching a dedicated online learning portal and organizing a series of faculty development programmes, seminars, and workshops. The reopening of the University post-pandemic witnessed a strengthened resolve, with the MAC community seamlessly integrating acquired digital proficiencies into everyday pedagogy. MAC’s infrastructure supports its vision: smart classrooms equipped with NET-enabled interactive panels, spacious auditoriums and conference halls, ICT-enabled laboratories, a well-stocked digital library, a fully functional gymnasium, an oncampus girls’ hostel

MAC’s infrastructure supports its vision: smart classrooms equipped with NET-enabled interactive panels, spacious auditoriums and conference halls, ICT-enabled laboratories, a well-stocked digital library, a fully functional gymnasium, an oncampus girls’ hostel that ensures a secure residential experience for outstation students, an experiential space, Sahastradhara where real-life learning and theoretical knowledge intersects. Several specialized centres within the college—including the Media Centre, Centre for Civic Education, Centre for Performing Arts and Cultural Studies, and Sanskriti Kala Kendra—contribute to students’ holistic growth. The Abdul Kalam Centre for Research and the newly instituted Vivekananda Study Circle, further MAC’s efforts to embed a robust research culture among its students and faculty. The college is also proud to serve as a centre for IGNOU and the Non-Collegiate Women’s Education Board (NCWEB). Student creativity and expression flourish through the college’s wide array of co-curricular societies and departmental associations. Supported by enthusiastic faculty mentors, these platforms instil values of integrity, hard work, and perseverance. The college remains deeply committed to inclusivity, actively supporting students from all backgrounds. Flagship student-led initiatives such as NSS projects—AKSHAR, SYAHI, ABHI, SAMARTHYA, and others—reflect MAC’s enduring sense of social responsibility. The Girls’ NCC unit, in particular, has brought honour to the college through its outstanding performance and achievements. MAC’s enduring sense of social responsibility. The Girls’ NCC unit, in particular, has brought honour to the college through its outstanding performance and achievements. The college also takes pride in its vigorous promotion of sports, with many students representing the state and nation in diverse disciplines. MAC’s alumni have already distinguished themselves in various professional, civic, and cultural spheres. The Maharaja Agrasen College Alumni Association (MACAA) fosters this growing network through annual gatherings and initiatives that maintain a strong bond between alumni and their alma mater. MAC’s guiding philosophy is rooted in adaptability, resilience, and vision. With eyes set firmly on the future, it continues its mission to lead with excellence, embracing every challenge as an opportunity to scale new heights. MAC PROFILE

FACILITIES ICT Centre The Centre supports the e-curriculum needs of computer-based practical oriented subjects across various courses offered by the college. In addition, it operates a dedicated general-purpose computer lab, accessible to faculty, students, and administrative staff for their computing requirements at their convenience. This lab offers a diverse technological environment, equipped with Windows 7, Mac OS, and Linux operating systems. A secure Wi-Fi network is also provided by the Centre, ensuring seamless internet access for all members of the college community. Media Lab The College houses a fully equipped Media Centre, featuring high-definition technology and in-house professional expertise to provide comprehensive training in both pre-production and post-production for Journalism students. In addition to academic instruction, the Centre serves as a hub for film screenings, short filmmaking workshops, and seminars or conferences focused on diverse facets of media and journalism. Hallmarked by Live Projects and active Industry Interface programmes, the Media Centre plays a pivotal role in bridging classroom learning with real-world media experience. The College also collaborates with industry partners to host various events under the aegis of the Centre. Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel Auditorium Housed in a dedicated three-storey complex, the state-of-the-art auditorium stands as a cornerstone of the College’s cultural and extracurricular landscape. With a seating capacity of 700, it is named in honour of Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, the Iron Man of India, and serves as the primary venue for the institution’s most significant academic and cultural engagements. Vidyottama Girls’ Hostel Vidyottama, the Girls’ Hostel of Maharaja Agrasen College, provides a safe, secure, and comfortable residential environment for female students. Spread across three floors, the hostel accommodates 58 residents, with designated dining facilities located on the ground floor. Each floor is equipped with a pantry featuring a water cooler, washing machine, microwave, induction stove, ironing amenities, and other essential conveniences.

FACILITIES Admissions are granted strictly on the basis of merit, in adherence to the prevailing reservation norms. Prospective outstation students are advised to visit the college website—www.mac.du.ac.in—regularly for the latest updates and details regarding hostel admissions and related procedures. Sports Facilities Maharaja Agrasen College offers a comprehensive array of sports facilities, along with professional coaching and training in disciplines such as Athletics, Archery, Basketball, Badminton, Chess, Cricket, Cycling, Soccer, Korfball, Softball, Volleyball, and Wushu. The College Sports Committee oversees all sporting activities and regularly organises a wide range of sports and adventure programs for students, faculty, and non-teaching staff alike. A major highlight is the annual inter-college sports festival SPARDHA, which fosters healthy competition and camaraderie across institutions. To further promote physical wellness, the College houses a fully equipped gymnasium—the MAC Wellness Centre—accessible to all students and staff during morning hours. For hostel residents, the facility remains available during the evenings as well, offering greater flexibility. Looking ahead, the College envisions several new developments to expand its fitness infrastructure: Library At MAC, the Library serves as an intellectually vibrant hub, fostering cognitive growth and academic exploration. Spanning two floors, it offers a thoughtfully designed environment with comfortable seating for both students and faculty. The collection boasts over 44,668 books, approximately 135,000 e-books (accessible via login), and more than 5,000 e-journals, along with a rich array of digital resources in Science and Technology, Social Sciences, Arts and Humanities, Commerce, and Management. The Library also houses an audio-visual collection of around 200

students and faculty. The collection boasts over 44,668 books, approximately 135,000 e-books (accessible via login), and more than 5,000 e-journals, along with a rich array of digital resources in Science and Technology, Social Sciences, Arts and Humanities, Commerce, and Management. The Library also houses an audio-visual collection of around 200 CDs and DVDs. Fully automated through KOHA—an open-source integrated library management system, it is seamlessly organized and user-friendly. Subscriptions to N-LIST (National Library and Information Services Infrastructure for Scholarly Content) and the Delhi University Library System (DULS) further extend access to thousands of e-resources through secure login credentials. Smart Class Rooms Maharaja Agrasen College took a significant step toward advancing next-generation pedagogy by establishing six state-of-the-art smart classrooms on its campus. Aligned with the college’s vision of delivering holistic and high-quality education, these classrooms have transformed the teaching-learning environment into a more dynamic and inclusive space. Equipped with real-time e-learning tools—such as audio-visual aids, multimedia content, online conferences, Power Point presentations, and 2D and 3D animations, these classrooms have enriched both virtual and physical learning experiences. This modern setup seamlessly integrates traditional classroom methods with digital platforms, making education more accessible, interactive, and engaging for all students. ‘Sahastradhara’—A Nexus of Thought and Learning Inaugurated on January 15, 2025, Sahastradhara stands as a vibrant confluence of diverse intellectual streams—a space envisioned by the College to foster critical thinking and meaningful dialogue among students. Conceived in alignment with the principles of NEP 2020, this informal, open learning environment recognises the transformative potential

Thought and Learning Inaugurated on January 15, 2025, Sahastradhara stands as a vibrant confluence of diverse intellectual streams—a space envisioned by the College to foster critical thinking and meaningful dialogue among students. Conceived in alignment with the principles of NEP 2020, this informal, open learning environment recognises the transformative potential of experiential education beyond traditional classrooms.

CELLS/CENTRES To enrich this dynamic space, several thoughtfully designed features have been introduced: a herbal garden, yoga and meditation parks, vermi and anaerobic composting pits, organic farming beds, seedling plots, a dedicated sowing area, a polyhouse, and a gazebo. Together, these elements cultivate not only ecological awareness but also serve as micro-learning centres tailored to the needs of Skill Enhancement Courses (SEC) and Value-Added Courses (VAC), offering immersive, hands-on learning opportunities. Himalayan Study Circle: Exploring the Spirit of the Mountains Established in August 2022, the Himalayan Study Circle (HSC@MAC) was envisioned as a multi-disciplinary initiative dedicated to the exploration of Himalayan ecology and cultural heritage. Affiliated with the Centre for Himalayan Studies, University of Delhi, the Study Circle engages in research spanning Himalayan history, geography, society, economy, polity, culture, traditional knowledge, bio-resources, environment, and sustainable development. Its engagements include: Fortnightly discussions on diverse themes: geomorphology, cultural landscapes, biodiversity, habitats, and regional traditions Documentary screenings featuring globally acclaimed ecological and cultural narratives Nature walks and field visits to biodiversity parks, national parks, and wildlife sanctuaries Annual Himalayan Festival, celebrated at both college and university levels Compilation of an Anthology on Himalayan Culture, capturing the multifaceted essence of the region Through these activities, HSC@MAC nurtures a spirit of inquiry, appreciation, and preservation, and verbalises the majestic Himalayas into the academic and cultural life of the institution. College Publications The academic community at Maharaja Agrasen College recognises the importance of fostering in-house publications as a means of encouraging scholarly and creative expression. activities, HSC@MAC nurtures a spirit of inquiry, appreciation, and preservation, and verbalises the majestic Himalayas into the academic and cultural life of the institution. College Publications The academic community at Maharaja Agrasen College recognises the importance of fostering in-house publications as a means of encouraging scholarly and creative expression. Among its notable initiatives is The Indian Journal of Social Enquiry, a distinguished research journal that has secured a prominent place in the landscape of academic publishing across the country. The Department of Business Economics contributes to this vibrant culture through its annual publication, BEAM, which serves as a platform for students to articulate their academic insights and engage with contemporary business trends. Similarly, the Department of Journalism regularly brings out its newsletter, MAC VOICE, while the Department of English publishes Edges, a creative writing journal dedicated to diverse student expressions. Remarkably, Edges is entirely conceptualised, edited, and designed by students, offering them a comprehensive understanding of the journalistic process from content creation to publication. The college magazine, AGRANIKA, stands as a testament to the collective spirit of the college community. This magazine designed by the Magazine and Prospectus Committee of the college provides a vivid canvas for creativity and serves as a chronicle of the academic year. Published annually, AGRANIKA is formally released during the college’s Annual Day celebration. National Service Scheme (NSS) The National Service Scheme (NSS) stands as a beacon of youth-driven social commitment, devoted to nurturing a sense of civic responsibility and service among students. Inspired by the guiding mantra (“May all be happy”) and harmonised

CELLS/CENTRES with the national ethos of ¶ ** and , NSS empowers young minds to contribute meaningfully toward building an inclusive, equitable society. Ongoing Projects and Initiatives Project ABHI (Agrasen Baroji Help Initiative): MAC’s NSS unit has adopted the villages of Baroji (Haryana) and Nistauli (U.P.) to advance rural development and empowerment. Through consistent field visits, volunteers educate residents about governance, civic rights, and self-reliance, ensuring the villagers are not merely recipients of help but function as active change-makers within their communities. Project Akshar: Focused on holistic education for underprivileged children in Dallupura and Kondli, this initiative blends academic support with lessons in life skills, hygiene, and confidence-building. Volunteers also host joyful community events like Diwali Melas and Children’s Day celebrations to nurture emotional and social well-being. AAROGYA: A public health drive that raises awareness about preventive healthcare and encourages wellnessoriented habits among communities. Swachhata: Aligned with the Swachh Bharat Abhiyan initiated by the Government of India, this flagship program promotes environmental cleanliness through regular drives, waste management efforts, and awareness campaigns. AGRANI: Focused on empowering women, this initiative addresses gender inequality by creating opportunities for skill development, expression, and growth. SYAAHI: A creative platform where volunteers voice social messages through reflective writing to promote peace, equity, and shared humanity. SANJAAL: The tech wing of NSS, where volunteers harness digital tools to spread awareness via posters, reels, and online campaigns. This wing recognises the dynamic role of technology in influencing the masses to lead to a positive change in society.

National Cadet Corps (NCC) The National Cadet Corps (NCC) is a voluntary youth organization under the Indian Armed Forces, committed to shaping young individuals into disciplined and patriotic citizens. Our college unit, MAC-NCC, operates under the 3 Delhi Girls Battalion and currently comprises approximately 110 girl cadets. Despite being a relatively new unit, MAC-NCC has demonstrated commendable enthusiasm and initiative. Our cadets actively participate in a variety of social and environmental initiatives, including cleanliness drives, tree plantation campaigns, and the adoption and upkeep of public statues. The unit also organizes impactful events such as lectures, awareness talks, and commemorations of International Yoga Day,

MAC-NCC has demonstrated commendable enthusiasm and initiative. Our cadets actively participate in a variety of social and environmental initiatives, including cleanliness drives, tree plantation campaigns, and the adoption and upkeep of public statues. The unit also organizes impactful events such as lectures, awareness talks, and commemorations of International Yoga Day, Environment Day, and other significant occasions. In recent years, our cadets have proudly represented the unit in several prestigious national-level events and camps, including the All India Annual NCC Trek (Darjeeling and Ajmer), Prime Minister’s Rally, Special National Integration Camp, Thal Sainik Camp, Independence Day Camp, Youth Exchange Program, Ek Bharat Shreshtha Bharat Camp, and Mountaineering Training Camp, among others.

SC/ST/OBC Cell (As per UGC Guidelines) The college has established an SC/ST/OBC Cell to support students from these categories. Programme – 230 seats Aspiring candidates are encouraged to visit the official NCWEB website for details on the admission process and course offerings. Sanskriti Kala Kendra The Department of Hindi at Maharaja Agrasen College proudly established the Sanskriti Kala Kendra with the vision of nurturing the holistic development of students. Beyond academic instruction, the Kendra is committed to instilling cultural sensitivity and ethical values among learners. Notably, Maharaja Agrasen College has launched a long-term educational and cultural exchange programme with Tokyo University of Foreign Studies, Tokyo, Japan—fostering international dialogue and exposure. To further its objectives, the Centre organises a vibrant array of activities including educational trips, cultural exchange programmes, and seminars, symposia, and expert lectures on pressing socio-cultural themes. These initiatives aim to provide students with a well-rounded and enriching educational experience. Civic Education Centre The Civic Education Centre is dedicated to cultivating constitutional values and responsible citizenship through purposeful learning. Central to its mission is the Leadership Development Camp, alongside various civic initiatives across Delhi that encourage student leadership and active engagement. To empower educators, the Centre develops and delivers Teacher Training Programmes in Civic Education, while also working on: Designing and implementing monitoring and evaluation mechanisms, Publishing research material, Developing standards and curriculum frameworks and Running

Development Camp, alongside various civic initiatives across Delhi that encourage student leadership and active engagement. To empower educators, the Centre develops and delivers Teacher Training Programmes in Civic Education, while also working on: Designing and implementing monitoring and evaluation mechanisms, Publishing research material, Developing standards and curriculum frameworks and Running leadership and network training programmes Its outreach strategy involves fostering sustained collaboration with key stakeholders, including the Department of Education, schools, colleges, homes, and local government officials—to collectively strengthen India’s civic fabric. Workshops are regularly conducted to instil awareness among students about their rights and duties, and to familiarise them with the constitutional provisions that shape democratic life. The Centre also passionately advocates for social justice and environmental stewardship, engaging students and the wider community in programmes that support the restoration of natural spaces, including parks and rivers. Gandhi Study Circle The Gandhi Study Circle is dedicated to exploring the life, ideas, and transformative actions of Mahatma Gandhi. Committed to making Gandhian thought relevant to contemporary times, the Study Circle engages participants through a diverse array of activities held throughout the year, such as cleanliness drives, reading circles, discussion forums, quizzes, creative writing sessions, and debate competitions. The Women Development Cell is an initiative dedicated to empowering women and fostering gender equality. Every year the Cell organises numerous events such as, awareness programs, health camps, and skill-building workshops, to address women's issues and promote holistic development. Through collaborations with experts and active student participation, the Cell creates a platform for dialogue, learning, and action. Any details pertaining to its activities will be uploaded on the College website. Environment Cell: G-ou The Environment Cell of Maharaja Agrasen College (G-one) is dedicated to fostering environmental awareness and implementing proactive solutions to address contemporary ecological challenges. Through its diverse activities, the Cell nurtures an ethically conscious and environmentally responsible mindset among all members of the college community. A significant achievement was realised when the College was declared a Zero Waste Campus, following successful collaborations with the Indian Pollution Control Association (IPCA) and Jaagruti Waste Paper Recyclers. These partnerships have enabled systematic waste management practices—including the handling of paper, plastic, and e-waste.

The details regarding activities and information related to the membership of the Environment Cell: G-ou will be uploaded on the college website. Training and Placement Cell (TPC) In a rapidly globalising economy, securing a strong foothold in the professional world has become increasingly competitive. The Training and Placement Cell (TPC) of Maharaja Agrasen College plays a vital role in bridging the gap between academic life and professional success by offering students a unified platform for growth and opportunity. TPC’s mission is to empower students through: 1. Employment and internship opportunities; 2. Development of communication and management skills; 3. Cultivation of corporate etiquette and professionalism. To inspire and empower, TPC hosts numerous workshops and guest sessions featuring accomplished entrepreneurs and professionals whose journeys encourage students to aim higher and dream bigger. It also conducts regular career counselling webinars, helping individuals identify their unique strengths and develop the mindset to become standout achievers. Members of the Cell possess a deep understanding of corporate dynamics and foster a culture of excellence. Equal Opportunity Cell (EOC) At Maharaja Agrasen College, the Equal Opportunity Cell (EOC) is committed to fostering an inclusive and empathetic campus environment. Recognising persons with disabilities as valuable contributors to society, the Cell works tirelessly to promote awareness and sensitivity around issues of disability. EOC plays an instrumental role in: Facilitating counselling services and accessible pedagogy, Introducing assistive technologies and Ensuring infrastructural accessibility across campus To integrate individuals with disabilities into the heart of college life, EOC regularly organises a spectrum of engaging activities throughout the year: Disability sensitization workshops, Expert lectures and skill development sessions, Job fairs dedicated to inclusivity and Sports and cultural programs. The Cell’s efforts are rooted in the belief that disability is not a limitation, but a vital aspect of human diversity—deserving of dignity, opportunity, and representation in every space. CELLS/CENTRES mindset necessary for future success. It focuses on nurturing competencies that go hand-in-hand with innovative infrastructure, preparing learners to thrive in an ever-evolving world. Institutional Support & Innovation Initiatives The Centre is dedicated to providing continuous infrastructural and IT assistance alongside brainstorming sessions with faculty members and industry professionals to nurture emerging ideas. Select ventures may also receive financial support. In previous academic years, the college has successfully completed 10 Innovation Projects and 7 Star Innovation Projects. To enhance academic engagement, the college also offers a range of short-term courses and operates an e-learning portal, ensuring a seamless and interactive teaching-learning environment. Short-Term Courses The college launched short-term courses to enrich the undergraduate curriculum, empower students to face post-graduation challenges, and strengthen their soft skills. Courses offered include: Introduction to Data Science for Business using R Programming, Data Analysis for Business Decision Making, Sugam Sangeet, Mobile App Designing: Level 1, English Skills for Professional Success: Level and Legal Awareness (in collaboration with East Delhi Legal Services Authority) New short-term courses are slated to begin in the 2025–25 academic session, further supporting students’ skill development and knowledge enhancement. It strives to deliver impactful training through hands-on experimentation, encouraging collaboration between students and educators in the field of embedded systems and robotics. Internal Quality Assurance Cell (IQAC) Established on October 3, 2015, in alignment with NAAC guidelines, the MAC-IQAC is committed to enhancing the college’s academic and research standards. It aims to build a holistic and consistent quality framework that drives institutional excellence. The cell comprises the Principal as Chairperson, department heads, and administrative officials. Together, they foster a culture of continuous improvement by institutionalizing best practices and internalizing quality standards across all academic and administrative functions. The Northeast Cell The Northeast Cell of Maharaja Agrasen College stands as a tribute to the rich cultural tapestry of India’s northeastern states. It provides a dynamic space for students from the region to showcase the ethos and heritage of the eight northeastern states through thoughtfully curated events and programs. With a focus on empowering students intellectually and creatively, the Cell mentors them into becoming socially responsible and visionary leaders. Rooted in the spirit of Ek Bharat Shreshtha Bharat, it educates peers from various parts of India on the unique cultures, traditions, and historical legacies of the Northeast. Over the years, the Northeast Cell has organised numerous impactful events, such as Northeast Cultural Fest cum Folk Dance Competition, essay, poster making and quiz competitions, etc. Through these initiatives, the Cell continues to be a fertile ground for fostering awareness, unity, and appreciation of India’s vibrant diversity. Students’ Union The Students' Union of Maharaja Agrasen College serves as the highest academic representative body for students, dedicated to their welfare and development. representative body for students, dedicated to their welfare and development. Functioning in close coordination with the University, the Union plays a pivotal role in organising academic and co-curricular activities that enrich campus life. It is a democratically elected body, with selection based on academic excellence, regular attendance, sincerity, and demonstrated leadership qualities. In collaboration with faculty members of respective departments of the college and the Student Advisory Committee, the Union actively contributes to the enhancement of college infrastructure and facilities, striving to create a more conducive academic environment. Beyond administration, the Students’ Union cultivates teamwork, encourages innovative thinking, and translates collective ideas into meaningful action and long-term vision. Student Advisory Committee The Student Advisory Committee (SAC) plays a pivotal role in guiding the Students’ Union and providing a broad range of opportunities for student participation in academic and co-curricular pursuits. Established in 2013, the Committee began its journey with the inauguration of the first National Student Academic Congress on the theme “Educational Reforms: The Way Forward.” Since 2016, SAC has also been instrumental in facilitating the democratic election of the College’s Students’ Union, in accordance with the University of Delhi’s guidelines and the Lyngdoh Commission’s recommendations. This initiated a new era of student leadership and representation at Maharaja Agrasen College, beginning with the election of its first student-led body. As a vital institutional body, SAC not only supervises the overall functioning of the Students’ Union but also serves as a bridge between student representatives and the college administration. in Maharaja Agrasen College. For admission to the College UG Programs, a candidate has to apply in Common Seat Allocation System (CSAS-2025) of University of Delhi. INFORMATION Step III: Seat Allocation The University of Delhi will provisionally allocate seats to applicants during the various allocation rounds. The applicants must ‘Accept’ the allocated seat and apply for admission to MAC. The college will then verify the documents and approve the admission. For details, please refer to CSAS - 2025 document available at: https:// admission.uod.ac.in Step IV: Selection of Course and College Upon seat allocation, applicants must log in to their Dashboards to accept the program and college allocated. i. Please note that ‘Accept’ option to apply in the college as per allocated seat can be made only online through their Dashboard on the UG admissions portal. There will be no physical visit to the colleges at this point in the process. ii. The procedure for accepting the allocated seat by the applicant must be completed within the stipulated time period. Step V: Online verification of documents by respective colleges The college shall verify the documents uploaded by the applicant for the program-specific eligibility, minimum eligibility and category. Those approved are required to pay the fee and receive a confirmation of their admission. Step VI: Payment of fees to confirm admission Once the Principal of the College has approved their admission, the applicants will receive a link on their Dashboard through which they must submit the program fees due. UNIVERSITY ORDINANCES REGARDING CONDUCT AND DISCIPLINE ORDINANCE XV-B: Maintenance of Discipline among Students of the University 1. All powers relating to discipline and disciplinary actions are vested in the Vice-Chancellor. 2. The Vice-Chancellor may delegate all or such powers as he/she deems proper to the Proctor and to such other persons as he/ she may specify in this behalf. 3. Without prejudice to the generality of power to enforce discipline under the Ordinance the following shall amount to acts of gross indiscipline: a. Physical assault, or threat to use physical force, against any member of the teaching and non-teaching staff of any Institution/Department and against any student within the University of Delhi a. Carrying of, use of or threat to use of any weapons b. Any violation of the provisions of the Civil Rights Protection Act, 1976 c. Violation of the status, dignity and honour of students belonging to the scheduled castes and tribes. d. Any practice-whether verbal or otherwise-derogatory of women. e. Any attempt at bribing or corruption in any manner. f. Wilful destruction of institutional property. g. Creating ill-will or intolerance on religious or communal grounds. h. Causing disruption in any manner of the academic functioning of the University system. i. Prohibition of Ragging as per Ordinance XV-C. 2. on religious or communal grounds. h. Causing disruption in any manner of the academic functioning of the University system. i. Prohibition of Ragging as per Ordinance XV-C. 2. Without prejudice to the generality of his/her powers relating to the maintenance of discipline and taking such action in the interest of maintaining discipline as may seem to him/her appropriate, the Vice-Chancellor, may in the exercise of his/ her powers aforesaid order or direct that any student or students: a. be expelled; or\ a. be, for a stated period rusticated; or b. be not for a stated period, admitted to a course or courses of study in a College, Department, or Institution of the University; or

INFORMATION c. be fined with a sum of rupees that may be specified; or d. be debarred from taking a university or College or Departmental Examination or Examination for one or more years; or e. that the result of the student or students concerned in the examination or Examinations in which he/she or they have appeared be cancelled. 2. The Principals of the Colleges, Heads of the Halls, Deans of Faculties, Heads of Teaching Departments in the University, the Principal, School of Open Learning and Librarians shall have the authority to exercise all such disciplinary powers over students in their respective Colleges, Institutions, Faculties and Teaching Departments in the University as may be necessary for the proper conduct of the Institutions, Halls and teaching in the concerned Departments. They may exercise their authority through, or delegate authority to such of the teachers in their Colleges, Institutions or Departments as they may specify for these purposes. 3. Without prejudice to the powers of the Vice-Chancellor and the Proctor as aforesaid, detailed ruled of discipline and proper conduct shall be framed. 4. These rules may be supplemented, where necessary, by the Principals of Colleges, Heads of Halls, Deans of Faculties and Heads of Teaching Departments in this University. Each student shall be expected provide himself/herself with a copy of these rules. 5. At the time of admission, every student shall be required to sign a declaration that on admission he/she submits himself/ herself to the disciplinary jurisdiction of the Vice-Chancellor and several

of Teaching Departments in this University. Each student shall be expected provide himself/herself with a copy of these rules. 5. At the time of admission, every student shall be required to sign a declaration that on admission he/she submits himself/ herself to the disciplinary jurisdiction of the Vice-Chancellor and several authorities of the University who may be vested with the authority to exercise discipline under the Acts, the Statutes, the Ordinances and the rules that have been framed therein by the University. ORDINANCE XV-C: Prohibition and Punishment for Ragging 1. Ragging in any form is strictly prohibited, within the premises of College/Department or Institution and any part of Delhi University system as well as on public transport. 2. Any individual or collective act or practice of ragging constitutes gross indiscipline and shall be dealt with under this Ordinance. 3. Ragging for the purposes of this Ordinance, ordinarily means any act, conduct or practice by which dominant power or status of senior students is brought to bear on students freshly enrolled or students who are in any way considered junior or inferior by other students; and includes individual or collective acts or practices which-Involve physical assault or threat to use of physical force a. Violate the status, dignity and honour of women students b. Violate the status, dignity and honour of students belonging to the scheduled caste and tribe c. Expose students to ridicule and contempt and affect their self-esteem d. Entail verbal abuse and aggression, indecent gestures and obscene behaviour.

physical force a. Violate the status, dignity and honour of women students b. Violate the status, dignity and honour of students belonging to the scheduled caste and tribe c. Expose students to ridicule and contempt and affect their self-esteem d. Entail verbal abuse and aggression, indecent gestures and obscene behaviour. 2. The Principal of a College, the Head of the Department or an Institution, the authorities of College, or University Hostel or Halls of Residence shall take immediate action on any information of the occurrence of ragging. 3. Notwithstanding anything in Clause (4) above, the Proctor may also suomoto enquire into any incident of ragging and make a report to the Vice-Chancellor of the identity of those who have engaged in ragging and the nature of the incident. 4. The Proctor may also submit an initial report establishing the identity of the perpetrators of ragging and the nature of the ragging incident. 5. If the Principal of a College or Head of the Department or Institution or the Proctor is satisfied that for some reason, to be recorded in writing, it is not reasonably practical to hold such an enquiry, he/she may so advise the Vice-Chancellor accordingly. 6. When the Vice-Chancellor is satisfied that it is not expedient to hold such an enquiry, his/her decision shall be final. 7. On the receipt of a report under Clause (5) or (6) or a determination by the relevant authority under clause (7) disclosing the occurrence of ragging incidents described in Clause 3(a),

Vice-Chancellor is satisfied that it is not expedient to hold such an enquiry, his/her decision shall be final. 7. On the receipt of a report under Clause (5) or (6) or a determination by the relevant authority under clause (7) disclosing the occurrence of ragging incidents described in Clause 3(a), (b) and (c), the Vice-Chancellor shall direct or order rustication of a student or students for a specific number of years.

INFORMATION 8. The Vice-Chancellor may in other cases of ragging order or direct that any student or students be expelled or be not for a stated period, admitted to a course of study in a college, departmental examination for one or more years or that the results of the student or students concerned in the examination or examinations in which they appeared be cancelled. 9. In case any students who have obtained degrees or diplomas of Delhi University are found guilty; under this Ordinance, appropriate action will be taken under Statute 15 for withdrawal of degrees or diploma conferred by the University. 10. For the purpose of this Ordinance, abetment to ragging whether by way of any act, practice or incitement of ragging will also amount to ragging. 11. All Institutions within the Delhi University system shall be obligated to carry out instruction/directions issued under this Ordinance, and to give aid assistance to the Vice-Chancellor to achieve the effective implementation of the Ordinance. Note: Order of the Vice-Chancellor in pursuance of Ordinance XV-C: Where incident (s) of ragging are reported to the Vice-Chancellor by any authority under this Ordinance, the students (s) involved in ragging, shall be expelled for a specified term, designated in the order. Non-students involved in reports of ragging will be proceeded with under the criminal law of India; they will also be rendered ineligible for period of five years from seeking enrolment in any of the institutions of the University of Delhi. WHEREAS sexual harassment results in violation of the fundamental rights of a woman to equality under articles 14 and 15 of the Constitution of India and her right to life and to live with dignity

prevention and redressal of complaints of sexual harassment and for matters connected therewith incidental thereto. WHEREAS sexual harassment results in violation of the fundamental rights of a woman to equality under articles 14 and 15 of the Constitution of India and her right to life and to live with dignity under article 21http://www.shebox.nic.in/assets/site/ main/images/Sexual-Harassment-at-Workplace-Act.pdf. of the Constitution and right to practice any profession or to carry on any occupation, trade or business which includes a right to a safe environment free from sexual harassment. AND WHEREAS the protection against sexual harassment and the right to work with dignity are universally recognized human rights by international conventions and instruments such as Convention on the Elimination of all forms of discrimination against Women, which has been ratified on the 25th June, 1993 by the Government of India.

INFORMATION AND WHEREAS it is expedient to make provisions for giving effect to the said Convention for protection of women against sexual harassment at workplace. Section 9 - Complaint of sexual harassment 1. Any aggrieved woman may make in writing a complaint of sexual harassment at workplace to the Internal Committee within a period of three months from the date of incident and in case of a series of incidents within a period of three months from the date of last incident: Provided that where such complaint cannot be made in writing, the Presiding officer or any member of the Internal Committee shall render all reasonable assistance to the woman for making the complaint in writing: Provided further that the Internal Committee may for the reasons to be recorded in writing, extend the time limit not exceeding three months, if it is satisfied that the circumstances were such which prevented the woman from filing a complaint within the said period. Where the aggrieved woman is unable to make a complaint on account of her physical or mental incapacity or death or otherwise, her legal heir or such other person as may be prescribed may make a complaint under this section. Section 14 - Punishment for false or malicious complaint and false evidence. 1. Where the Internal Committee arrives at a conclusion that the allegation against the respondents is malicious or the aggrieved woman or any other person making the complaint has made the complaint knowing it to be false or

Section 14 - Punishment for false or malicious complaint and false evidence. 1. Where the Internal Committee arrives at a conclusion that the allegation against the respondents is malicious or the aggrieved woman or any other person making the complaint has made the complaint knowing it to be false or the aggrieved woman or any other person making the complaint has produced any forged or misleading document, it may recommend to the employer or the District Officer as the case may be to take action against the woman or the person who has made the complaint under sub-section (1) or sub-section (2) of section 9 as the case may be, in accordance with the provisions of the service rules applicable to her or him or where no such service rules exist, in such manner as may be prescribed: Provided that a mere inability to substantiate a complaint or provide adequate proof need not attract action against the complainant under this section: Provided further that the malicious intent on parts of the complainant shall be established after an inquiry in accordance with the procedure prescribed, before any action is recommended. Where the Internal Committee arrives at a conclusion that during the inquiry any witness has given false evidence or produced any forged or misleading document, it may recommend to the employer of the witness or the District Officer as the case may be to take action in accordance with the provisions of the service rules applicable to the said witness or

inquiry any witness has given false evidence or produced any forged or misleading document, it may recommend to the employer of the witness or the District Officer as the case may be to take action in accordance with the provisions of the service rules applicable to the said witness or where no such service rules exist in such manner as may be prescribed. For further details, please see the website http: indiacode.nic.in/acts-in-pdf/142013.pdf. Redress of Grievance of Students As per UGC notification dated 6.5.19, grievance means complaints made by an aggrieved student in report of the following: Admission contrary to merit determined in accordance with the declared admission policy of the institution. i. Irregularity in the process under the declared admission policy of the institution. ii. Refusal admit in accordance with the declared admission policy of the institution. iii. Non-publication of prospectus by the institution in accordance with the provisions of these regulations. iv. Publication by the institution of any information in the prospectus, which is false or misleading and not based on facts. v. Withholding of, or refusal to return, any document in the form of certificates or degree, diploma or any other award or other document deposited by a student for the purpose of seeking admission in such institution, with a view to induce or compel such student to pay any fee or fees in respect or any course or program of study which such student does not intend to pursue. vi. Demand of money in excess of that

student for the purpose of seeking admission in such institution, with a view to induce or compel such student to pay any fee or fees in respect or any course or program of study which such student does not intend to pursue. vi. Demand of money in excess of that specified to be charged in the declared admission policy of the institution. vii. Violation by the institution or any law for the time being in force to regard to reservation of seats in admission to different category of students. viii. Non-payment or delay in payment of scholarships or financial aid admissible to any students under the declared admission policy of such institution, or under the conditions, if any, prescribed by the Commission.

INFORMATION ix. Delay by the institution in the conduct or examinations, or declaration of results, beyond the schedule specified in the academic calendar of the institution, or in such calendar prescribed by the Commission. x. Failure by the institution to provide student amenities as set out in the prospectus or is required to be extended by the institution under any provisions of law for the time being in force. xi. Non-transparent or unfair practices adopted by the institution for the evaluation of students. xii. Delay in, or denial of, the refund of fees due to a student who withdraws admission within the time mentioned in the prospectus or as may be notified by the Commission. xiii. Complaints of alleged discrimination of students from the Scheduled Caste, the Scheduled Tribes, Other Backward Classes, Women Minority or persons with disabilities categories. xiv. Denial of quality education as promised at the time of admission or required to be provided xv. Harassment or victimization of a student, other than cases of harassment, which are to be proceeded against under the penal provisions of any law for the time being in force. An aggrieved student may contact the Grievance redressal committee. INFORMATION Attendance and Examination Related Rules a. A candidate for the Semester I/III/V Examination shall not be deemed to have satisfied the required conditions of attendance unless he has attended, in all the subjects taken together, not less than two-thirds of the lectures/practical/ presentations/tutorials required to be attended provided that a student of the Semester I/III/V who does not fulfil the required conditions of attendance, as above, but has attended, in all the subjects taken together, not less than 40 per cent of lectures/ practical/presentations during the respective semester, may at the discretion of the Principal of the College concerned, appear for the ensuing semester Examination; but such a candidate shall be required to make up the deficiency at lectures and practical’s, in the next semester of the same academic year. Provided that a student of the II/IV/VI semester who does not fulfil the required conditions of attendance as above, but has attended in all the subjects taken together, not less than 40 per cent of the lectures/practical/presentation/tutorials, held during the respective semester, may at the discretion of the college Principal , be allowed to appear at the ensuing examination provided that s/he makes up the deficiency of the said attendance by combining the attendance of the previous semester in the ensuing semester. Provided further that the Principal of the College concerned may permit a student to appear in an examination notwithstanding that the student has not fulfilled the attendance requirement, if in the opinion of the Principal, such student

attendance by combining the attendance of the previous semester in the ensuing semester. INFORMATION Annexure-1 1. The assessment pattern that shall be adopted for the purpose of assessment of courses that have been approved by the competent authorities under the UGCF 2022 shall be as follows: (a) In any course where 01 credit is attributed to ‘Tutorial’, an objective assessment process has to be developed as a credit earned by a student in a course will eventually lead to its deposition in the Academic Bank of Credit of such student. Therefore, it is imperative that each credit earned by a student is adequately assessed and recorded accordingly. (b) In view of the above, the components of Tutorial have been listed so that at least some of these activities (listed at point no. 3 below) may be picked up by a student, in consultation with the teacher, as per rules devised for this purpose. The activities conducted shall be assessed through continuous assessment. (i) Some of the activities that may be done as component of tutorials are as follows: Literature review Book review Movie review Project activity (group) Research cum presentation Creative Writing/Paper writing Group discussion Problem Solving exercises Any creative production (may be done in a group) Innovative Project Any other scholastic work related to application of conceptual understanding of the subject. (ii) Further, out of forty marks allocated for Continuous Assessment of tutorial, five marks shall be for attendance, which shall be distributed as follows: a. More than 67% attendance but less than 70% attendance – 1 mark b. More than 70%

related to application of conceptual understanding of the subject. (ii) Further, out of forty marks allocated for Continuous Assessment of tutorial, five marks shall be for attendance, which shall be distributed as follows: a. More than 67% attendance but less than 70% attendance – 1 mark b. More than 70% attendance but less than 75% attendance – 2 marks c. More than 75% attendance but less than 80 % attendance – 3 marks d. More than 80% attendance but less than 85% attendance – 4 marks e. More than 85 % attendance – 5 marks There shall not be marks for attendance in Continuous Assessments for practical. 2. (i) Internal Assessment (IA) shall consist of marks secured in class tests, assignments/ presentations and attendance. For instance, for an Internal Assessment of 25 marks, Class tests shall comprise of 10 marks, Assignments/Presentations/Project/ Activity done as component of the Tutorial shall comprise of 10 marks and attendance shall be for 5 marks. Annexure-80 Item No. 4-1 (4-1-18) Internal Assessment Rules

INFORMATION Similarly, for IA of 30 marks, 6 marks shall be for attendance, 12 marks for class test and 12 marks for Assignments/Presentations/Project/ Activity. (ii) Six marks for attendance shall be distributed as follows: (a) More than 67% attendance but less than 70% attendance – 1.2 mark (b) More than 70% attendance but less than 75% attendance – 2.4 marks (c) More than 75% attendance but less than 80 % attendance – 3.6 marks (d) More than 80% attendance but less than 85% attendance – 4.8 marks (e) More than 85 % attendance – 6.0 marks 3. (i) To make Internal Assessment and Continuous Assessment fair and reasonable, it is necessary to strengthen the Monitoring Committee for Internal Assessment in every College, constituted as per clause 5 (ii) of Ordinance VIII-E of the Ordinances of the University. This Committee shall be responsible for the entire process of Internal Assessment in the College, including redressal of grievances, if any. The same Committee shall also look into the process of Continuous Assessment and redress grievances, if any. (ii) A student who is discontent with the decision taken by the Monitoring Committee for Internal Assessment of the College, with respect to the grievance submitted by him/her relating to Internal Assessment/Continuous Assessment, may file an appeal before an appellate body consisting of the Principal of the College, the Teacher-incharge of the department/senior faculty member, the concerned teacher who has assessed the aggrieved student and shall be chaired by the Nominee of Dean of Colleges

submitted by him/her relating to Internal Assessment/Continuous Assessment, may file an appeal before an appellate body consisting of the Principal of the College, the Teacher-incharge of the department/senior faculty member, the concerned teacher who has assessed the aggrieved student and shall be chaired by the Nominee of Dean of Colleges or Director South Delhi Campus, under whose jurisdiction the concerned College falls. 4. The theory examination and internal assessment shall be a cumulative assessment of the teaching-learning done in the theory as well as tutorial classes. 5. The assessment pattern for courses of different credit distributions is provided in Table -1. 6. Practical marks for courses of total four credits, shall comprise of (i) continuous evaluation (25%), (ii) end term practical examination (50%) and (iii) viva-voce (25%). Practical marks for courses of total two credits shall comprise of (i) continuous evaluation (50%), (ii) End term practical /written exam (25%) and (iii) viva-voce (25%) 7. The variance of the total marks in various combinations of LTP structure shall be aligned with the help of “weighted average” calculated through an appropriately devised formula for conversion of marks to grades.

8. “The attendance requirement for continuous mode of assessment shall be as per Ordinance VII of the Ordinances of the University”. 9. DEPARTMENTS The B.A. Programme at Maharaja Agrasen College is a dynamic, interdisciplinary course designed to meet the diverse academic and professional aspirations of students. Rooted in the flexibility of a choice-based framework, this course allows learners to explore a rich tapestry of subjects, including Economics, Political Science, History, Mathematics, Commerce, Computer Applications, Hindi, and English. It caters to students from varied academic backgrounds and equips them with the skills and insights essential for both public and private sector employment. At its core, the B.A. Programme fosters holistic development, placing academic growth at the forefront while offering a broad canvas of subjects tailored for those preparing for competitive examinations. Its multidisciplinary design ensures that students are not only immersed in foundational knowledge but also encouraged to cultivate a versatile intellectual toolkit. Taught by a diverse faculty across multiple disciplines, the course provides valuable opportunities for cross-disciplinary engagement and academic exploration. Students benefit from exposure to a wide range of ideas, perspectives, and discourses, which enrich their educational journey and prepare them for advanced specialization at the postgraduate level. The programme is especially well-suited for those aspiring to careers in academia and education, owing to its comprehensive structure and expansive subject choices—few courses offer such breadth and academic latitude. Central to student life in the B.A. Programme is LAKSHYA, the official society that embodies the spirit and energy of the course. LAKSHYA serves as a vibrant platform where students express their ideas, talents, and creativity through debates, quizzes, seminars, conferences, film screenings,

courses offer such breadth and academic latitude. Central to student life in the B.A. Programme is LAKSHYA, the official society that embodies the spirit and energy of the course. LAKSHYA serves as a vibrant platform where students express their ideas, talents, and creativity through debates, quizzes, seminars, conferences, film screenings, and other interactive events. The society also coordinates educational tours and excursions, adding a practical and experiential dimension to learning. Highly sought after under the National Education Policy (NEP), the B.A. Programme is a testament to interdisciplinary excellence. Its students continue to shine through active participation in college and university activities, exemplifying the intellectual versatility and communication prowess the course aims to foster. It is not merely a degree—it is a launchpad for a fulfilling and successful professional journey. B. A. Programme Dr. Mona Sinha (TIC, BA programme) sinhamona@mac.du.ac.in BA. Programme under NEP is an interdisciplinary and multifaceted programme, it lays the foundation of success for students by equipping them with knowledge and skills of different types.

DEPARTMENTS In today’s fast-paced and competitive global business landscape, organisations must harness managerial talent to optimise resource utilisation and enhance their competitive edge. The B.A. (Hons) Business Economics programme is designed to equip students with well-rounded managerial competencies—spanning human, technical, and conceptual dimensions. The course blends foundational knowledge of economics with core functional areas of business management, including marketing, finance, and operations research. It aims to nurture students into responsible, dynamic, ethical, and proactive future managers. The Department of Business Economics fosters inclusive learning and holistic development by promoting robust industry-academia engagement. Its student-industry interface programme brings accomplished professionals from the corporate world to share practical insights and mentorship regularly. To enrich students’ real-world exposure, the Department conducts various workshops and hosts events through its student society, the Business Economics Association (BEA). Signature annual events such as Entreuzest and Industrial Colloquium offer platforms for students to organise, lead, and participate—honing vital soft skills along with expertise in planning, marketing, and financial strategy. These simulations challenge students to perform under pressure, sparking critical thinking and showcasing analytical aptitude. Students are also encouraged to pursue academic research, often publishing papers under the expert guidance of faculty members. Emphasising experiential learning, the Department integrates project-based assessments throughout the course to reinforce practical application of theoretical knowledge. With a team of dedicated and experienced faculty members, the Department upholds its commitment to academic excellence. It ensures students graduate with the skills, insight, and confidence necessary to thrive in an evolving professional environment. B. A.

DEPARTMENTS B.A. Honours in English at Maharaja Agrasen College, is a highly sought-after course by new entrants and justly so. The syllabus consists of an entire panorama of literary texts engaging with different genres, contexts and critical tools to mobilize diverse discourses. Theoretical and practical approaches towards this spectrum of global literatures aims to train the learners in various methods of literary and sociocultural analyses. Research is encouraged in classroom teaching through initiatives such as individual and group research projects. Students and faculty have access to the departmental library, a growing digital archive through Learning Management systems (LMS) and a departmental website under construction. A full calendar of co-curricular activities, notified well in advance, is followed allowing young minds to connect with the wider world beyond the literary learning in their classroom ambit. ‘THE EDGES’, a mini literary magazine is a compilation of various literary creative writings by the students of English Honours. It is edited by a team of students under faculty supervision. The cocurricular events include the Annual Lecture Series, Literature Festival ‘LITERATI’, Meet the Author Series, various workshops and an excursion to places of cultural interest. Meet the Alumni series allows a meaningful interaction with Alumni of this course positioned successfully in their respective careers. Co-curricular Student Society, ‘ACTIVE’, lives up to its name each year by organising many well attended events under the guidance of their teachers. The department regularly organises national and international conferences which offer opportunities to students to interact with some of the

this course positioned successfully in their respective careers. Co-curricular Student Society, ‘ACTIVE’, lives up to its name each year by organising many well attended events under the guidance of their teachers. The department regularly organises national and international conferences which offer opportunities to students to interact with some of the best minds in the discipline as well as in allied fields. The NEP based structure and syllabi of DU English courses under UGCF scheme have been designed by incorporating inputs from well researched areas worldwide, and which provides these courses clarity of course statements, academic and job- oriented objectives, facilitating the achievements of course, essential readings, weekly teaching plans and keywords. To facilitate the achievements of course learning outcomes, the department applies the model of teaching-learning as included in syllabus which clearly demarcates a participatory three pillar approach consisting of 1. Course Learning Outcomes 2. Teaching and Learning Activity and 3. Assessment Tasks. To lay theoretical foundation for the course, the emphasis is on understanding concepts, expressing analytical approach through writing, and demonstrating conceptual and textual understanding in tests and exams. Teaching-learning activity comprises of interactive discussions in small groups in tutorial classes, how to think critically and write with clarity during assessment and discussions on exam questions and answering techniques. The assessment tasks include reading materials together in small groups, initiating discussion topics, participation in discussions, writing essay length assignments and class tests. The English Department is a stimulating, vibrant space, and which provides ample opportunities to students to
